en.extract:
Bannerman's shearwater (Puffinus bannermani) is a seabird in the family Procellariidae formerly considered conspecific with Audubon's shearwater (Puffinus lherminieri).


Range
Little is known of this species other than that it breeds in the Volcano Islands in the Ogasawara Group to the southwest of Japan and doesn't seem to range far from this area.


Population
The species population size has not been estimated but is assessed to be decreasing.


Diet and Habitat
Not much is known about this species and more research is required to obtain an understanding of its ecology. Based on the ecology of closely related species, P. bannermani is thought likely to feed on fish, squid and cephalopods, by surface-seizing, underwater pursuit, including diving and plunging, and pattering (del Hoyo et al. 1992, Brooke 2004). It is likely to nest in rock crevices or burrows and form colonies (del Hoyo et al. 1992).

'''Bannerman's shearwater''' ('''''Puffinus bannermani''''') is a [[seabird]] in the family [[Procellariidae]] formerly considered conspecific with '''[[Audubon's shearwater]]''' (''Puffinus lherminieri'').<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/loons/|title=''Loons, penguins, petrels''|publisher=International Ornithological Congress|access-date=2015-01-16}}</ref>

==Range==
Little is known of this species other than that it breeds in the [[Volcano Islands]] in the [[Ogasawara Group]] to the southwest of [[Japan]] and doesn't seem to range far from this area.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://maps.iucnredlist.org/map.html?id=22698272|title=''Puffinus bannermani range map''|publisher=International Union for Conservation of Nature|access-date=2015-01-16|archive-date=2023-11-02|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231102194046/http://maps.iucnredlist.org/map.html?id=22698272}}</ref>

==Population==
The species population size has not been estimated but is assessed to be decreasing.<ref name="iucn status 12 November 2021" />

==Diet and Habitat==
Not much is known about this species and more research is required to obtain an understanding of its ecology. Based on the ecology of closely related species, ''P. bannermani'' is thought likely to feed on [[fish]], [[squid]] and [[cephalopods]], by surface-seizing, underwater pursuit, including diving and plunging, and pattering (del Hoyo et al. 1992, Brooke 2004). It is likely to nest in rock crevices or burrows and form colonies (del Hoyo et al. 1992).<ref name="iucn status 12 November 2021" />
